video_title: Tech Stocks Hit By Losses as AI Concerns Spread |Bloomberg Businessweek Daily 6/23/2026
gist: Bloomberg Businessweek Daily (6/23, cross-posted) covers an AI/chip selloff triggered by SK Hynix/Samsung -10% (circuit breaker) on a report SK Hynix is slowing HBM for commodity DRAM: S&P -1.2%, Philly Semi -8% (still +90% YTD), Micron -12.7%. Sanctuary Wealth's Marianne Bartels calls it a sector-rotation correction (tech overbought, small caps/banks/biotech rotating in), not a peak, with a resilient consumer (+6% card spend). Oracle cut 21,000 jobs (some AI), Gensler says AI hyperscalers 'don't have the revenues,' SpaceX's $25B bond debut shows cash-flow concern in the short tranche, and Creation grows 50% while freezing call-center hiring.

insights.claim: A tech-led selloff hit US stocks (S&P -1.2%, Nasdaq -1.8%, Philadelphia Semiconductor Index -8% though still +90% YTD), with Micron -12.7%, SanDisk -14%, Nvidia -3.3% and Tesla -5.8% — triggered by SK Hynix and Samsung each plunging ~10% (circuit breaker) on a report SK Hynix is slowing HBM memory expansion and shifting to cheaper commodity DRAM.
insights.speaker: anchors (Massar/Stenovec)
insights.so_what: The selloff's catalyst is a supply-side HBM/DRAM signal from Korea — an AI-data-center-demand worry, not a broad macro shock.
insights.quote: SK Hynix is slowing expansion of our memory chip production and shifting emphasis to the cheaper commodity Dram

insights.claim: Despite the index drop, most S&P 500 names were higher (~310 up vs ~193 down) — a narrow, mega-cap-driven decline, which strategists read as healthy breadth beneath the surface.
insights.speaker: anchors
insights.so_what: Breadth holding up supports a rotation-not-crash interpretation of the tech selloff.

themes.theme: Sanctuary Wealth: rotation, not a peak

insights.claim: Sanctuary Wealth CIO Marianne Bartels says tech is extraordinarily overbought while the rest of the market is oversold — calling this a sector-rotation correction, not a major peak or the end of the tech bull, though semis could pull back 20-30% (after rising 200%+).
insights.speaker: Marianne Bartels (Sanctuary Wealth CIO)
insights.so_what: Treats the drop as a painful-but-contained rotation — a dip-buy frame for the broader market, with semis the most exposed.
insights.quote: tech is extraordinarily overbought relative to the rest of the market

insights.claim: Bartels sees leadership rotating into banks, biotech and small caps — calling small caps' 15-year bear market over and the start of a new secular bull — and reads domestically-focused small-cap strength (+20% YTD) as evidence the economy is doing well.
insights.speaker: Marianne Bartels
insights.so_what: A concrete rotation call — diversify from mega-cap tech into small caps/financials/biotech.
insights.quote: their bare market of 15 years is over

insights.claim: Bartels cites Bank of America card data showing consumer spending up ~6% YoY (~5% ex-gas), supported by wage gains at the middle/lower end plus savings drawdown and the tax bill.
insights.speaker: Marianne Bartels
insights.so_what: Hard card-data evidence of a resilient consumer underpinning the soft-landing case.

insights.claim: Bartels says new Fed chair Warsh's price-stability focus collapsed breakeven inflation yields toward 2%, pushed the 2-year yield up and sent the dollar to a breakout (highest since November) — and that Warsh may 'have the market do the work for him' with Greenspan-like ambiguity.
insights.speaker: Marianne Bartels
insights.so_what: Warsh is gaining inflation-fighting credibility — repricing breakevens, the front end and the dollar even without policy moves.

insights.claim: Oracle cut 21,000 jobs over the past 12 months (some eliminated by AI), shrinking headcount from 162,000 to 141,000 with ~$1.8B in restructuring costs.
insights.speaker: Amy Morris (Bloomberg)
insights.so_what: A concrete, quantified case of AI-driven white-collar headcount reduction at a megacap — the labor-substitution thesis made real.
insights.quote: Oracle cut 21,000 jobs in the past 12 months

insights.claim: Former SEC chair Gary Gensler argues AI hyperscalers and OpenAI must build real revenues and economy-wide productivity to justify valuations and let capital markets overlook the disruption — 'right now they don't have the revenues.'
insights.speaker: Gary Gensler
insights.so_what: Frames the central AI bull-case test: revenue and productivity must show up to validate the capex/valuations.
insights.quote: Right now they don't have the revenues

insights.claim: Creation CEO Kathryn Koester says AI's biggest ROI is in call centers/customer service via hybrid human+agentic workflows — her firm is growing ~50% YoY revenue while budgeting zero additional call-center/customer-service staff, instead hiring analysts, enterprise sales and architects.
insights.speaker: Kathryn Koester (Creation CEO)
insights.so_what: A live example of AI substituting frontline labor while shifting hiring up the value chain — the practical shape of AI ROI.
insights.quote: We're not budgeting a single additional person in our call center and customer service organization at all for this year

insights.claim: SpaceX's three-day selloff wiped more than $600B of market value; it is raising a debut $25B of bonds with ~$90B in orders at a premium over Treasuries, but the strongest demand was for the SHORTEST-dated tranche — reflecting concerns about SpaceX's cash flow.
insights.speaker: Amy Morris (Bloomberg)
insights.so_what: The bond market is pricing SpaceX cash-flow risk (front-loaded demand) even amid heavy oversubscription — a caution flag under the AI-compute story.
insights.quote: more than $600 billion from the company's market value

insights.claim: Cybersecurity firm Varonis is exploring a potential sale after takeover interest from PE firms including Blackstone, Thoma Bravo and Vista Equity Partners (shares +8%).
insights.speaker: Amy Morris (Bloomberg)
insights.so_what: Adds to the cybersecurity take-private/consolidation theme — PE appetite for the sector remains strong.

insights.claim: Latham Group's CEO offers a consumer/housing read: fiberglass pools are 25% of the US market and gaining share (6-day install vs 90-120 days for cement), the average North American pool costs ~$95,000, and homebuilders increasingly add pools as a mortgage-rate-buydown upgrade — with all US product made domestically.
insights.speaker: Sean Gaddis (Latham Group CEO)
insights.so_what: A discretionary-spend and housing barometer — plus a rate-buydown dynamic and a tariff-insulated domestic-manufacturing angle.
insights.quote: fiberglass makes up 25% of the US market

insights.claim: US composite PMI rose to 52.2 (fastest expansion in five months) on manufactured-goods demand, while gasoline averages $3.92/gal (up from under $2.80 to start the year) keeps political/affordability pressure on; the dollar is at its highest since November.
insights.speaker: Amy Morris / anchors
insights.so_what: Activity is accelerating even as gas prices stay elevated — a mixed macro backdrop feeding the dollar's strength.
